structured_payload_candidate.title: Fundamental Hydronic System Components
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The five fundamental components of a hydronic system are loads, source, expansion chamber, pump, and distribution system.

evidence_text:

design engineer provides complete information on the advantages
and disadvantages of ea ch system option. Just as the owner does not
usually know the relative advantages and disadvantages of differentHVAC systems, the design engineer  rarely knows all the owner’s
financial and functional goals.
judge_reason: Evidence provided does not match the candidate; it discusses owner goals and project scheduling, not hydronic system components.

structured_payload_candidate.title: System Design Step: Determine Loads
structured_payload_candidate.summary: First step in hydronic system design is to determine system and zone loads, which determine flow and affect heat transfer ability and energy performance.

structured_payload_candidate.title: System Design Step: Select Heat Transfer Devices
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Selecting comfort or process heat transfer devices (e.g., coils) has the single largest influence on hydronic system design.

structured_payload_candidate.title: System Design Step: Select Air Management
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Selecting an air management methodology is essential as all hydronic systems entrain air, and managing its collection is key to system pressure and safe operation.

structured_payload_candidate.title: System Design Step: Select Pump
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Proper pump selection is critical to limit energy use in transporting fluid through the distribution system; improper selection leads to inefficiency and poor performance.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Ceiling Plenum Return Air Temperature
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The air temperature in a return air plenum directly below a roof deck is usually 3 to 5°F higher during the air-conditioning season than in a ducted return.

evidence_text:
System selection per-
taining to HVAC security may require another design team member,a security consultant, and/or the owner’s own security group.
Equipment and System Constraints
Once  the goal criteria and addi tional goal options are verified,
equipment and system constraint s must be assessed and docu-
mented as part of the system an alysis and select ion recommendation
report. These constraints may include the following:
 Performance limitations (e.g., te mperature, humidity, space pres-
sure, etc.)
 Code updates and/or new codes
 Available capacity including e quipment size, as well as ductwork
and/or pipe size
 Available space and access in an d/or out with new or old equip-
ment
 Available utility source
 Energy budget (e.g., conceptual Btu/h·ft2 per year), code-driven
or targeted
 Equipment efficiency Operator knowledge and capabilities
 Existing building occupants Building architecture
Few projects allow detailed quantit ative evaluation of all alterna-
tives.
judge_reason: Evidence refers to security considerations, not return air plenum temperature differentials; no support for the claimed spec.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Underfloor Air Distribution (UFAD) Application
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Using a raised floor for air distribution (UFAD) is popular for computer rooms and cleanrooms and can reduce overall first cost and improve air circulation to the occupied area.

evidence_text:
Henc e, the owner must be proactive
and involved in system selection in  the conceptual phase of the job.
The same can be said for operator participation so that the finaldesign intent is sustainable and can continue to fulfill design intent.
All owners should request and/or require the design team to pro-
vide building and HVAC security (as defined in Chapter 61 of the2019 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications ). This security, in
addition to environmental health a nd safety criteria, should address
outside-the-building influences (e .g., smoke, toxic fumes, flood,
etc.) as well as influences inside the building.
judge_reason: Evidence discusses owner involvement in system selection, not UFAD applications; content mismatch.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Outdoor Air Intake Location for Security
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Consideration should be given to protecting outdoor air intakes against insertion of dangerous materials by locating the intakes on the roof or substantially above grade level.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Risk Assessment for Building Security
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The first consideration for protecting buildings against terrorist attacks via HVAC systems is to perform a risk assessment based on usage, size, population, and/or significance.
